chunk_summary: A clinic response to climate-related food insecurity using a two-minute screening tool to identify risks, stratify clinical priorities, and manage malnutrition, dehydration, and infection in children.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of climate shocks on child health, including weight faltering and recurrent infections.
Learn to implement a three-question food insecurity screening tool in clinical settings.
Recognize the importance of zinc supplementation and ORS in managing diarrhoeal illness in food-insecure children.
Identify follow-up strategies for children with weight faltering or reduced dietary diversity after climate events.
key_facts: A three-question screening tool assesses food insecurity in under two minutes.
Zinc supplementation guidelines: 10 mg/day for infants under 6 months and 20 mg/day for children 6–59 months, for 10–14 days.
Food insecurity amplifies risks of anaemia, growth decline, and recurrent infections.
Follow-up after illness or climate events should occur within 1–2 weeks for children with weight faltering or reduced dietary diversity.
ORS and zinc are affordable, high-impact interventions during diarrhoeal illness.
reference_quote: A three-question adaptation of the 'Hunger Vital Sign' logic can be used as a clinic entry screen: 'In the last 12 months, did you worry that food at home would run out before you had money to buy more?'
document_key_topics: Food insecurity screening in clinical settings
Impact of climate events on child health
Zinc supplementation and diarrhoeal illness management
